sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, Michelle, is a 4-year-old mother of twins who injured her right knee while skiing in Vermont last weekend.
She reports that she caught her ski and fell, causing her knee to pop and resulting in persistent pain.
She was unable to bear weight after the incident and was taken down the hill by the ski patrol.
The pain is localized to the outside of the knee, and she describes it as feeling like it will buckle.
She has difficulty bending and straightening the knee, but can do so somewhat with pain.
She denies numbness or tingling in her toes and can feel her toes normally.
She reports being generally healthy with no high blood pressure, diabetes, or other medical conditions.
Past medical history: [NOT MENTIONED]
Review of systems: [NOT MENTIONED]
Current medications: I prescribed ibuprofen 600-800 mg every 8 hours as needed for pain.
Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]
Physical exam: On examination of the right knee, I appreciate edema and an effusion, with pain to palpation of the lateral knee.
There is decreased flexion and extension, a positive Lachman sign, and palpable dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses.
There is no leg edema in the ankle.
Test Results: [NOT MENTIONED]
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient likely has a ruptured anterior cruciate ligament (ACL) in her right knee.
Problem list: ACL rupture; knee pain; knee instability.
Plan:
I ordered a knee MRI to confirm the diagnosis.
I will continue to have her take ibuprofen 600-800 mg every 8 hours as needed for pain.
I will also put her in a knee brace to provide additional support.
I will review the MRI results and discuss the next steps, including potential surgery, in a follow-up appointment.
Follow-up: I will see the patient again in a couple of days to discuss the MRI results and plan for further management.
